facts.title: Traditional markets
facts.fact: Traditional markets such as Seoul's Namdaemun and Gwangjang sell everything from clothes to street food. Friendly haggling is common at market stalls, though not in shops.
facts.korean_en: I buy fruit at the market.
facts.note: **깎아 주세요** = "Please give me a discount" (literally "please cut [the price]").

facts.title: Convenience stores do everything
facts.fact: Convenience stores are everywhere and offer far more than snacks — hot meals, package pickup, even simple banking. Online orders often arrive the very next morning.
facts.korean_en: I buy gimbap at the convenience store.
facts.note: **배달** (delivery) culture is huge — almost anything can be delivered to your door.

facts.title: The won, and a familiar face
facts.fact: The currency is the won. Coins run from 10 to 500 won and bills from 1,000 to 50,000 won. The 10,000-won note features King Sejong, who created Hangul.

facts.title: Department stores
facts.fact: Large department stores (백화점) like Lotte and Shinsegae are upscale destinations with fixed prices and polite staff. The basement food halls and rooftop gardens are attractions in themselves.
facts.korean_en: I bought clothes at the department store.
facts.note: **백화점** literally means "hundred goods store." Prices here are fixed, so don't haggle.

facts.title: Asking for something
facts.fact: The simplest way to ask for an item is **이거 주세요** — "this one, please." Point at what you want and the phrase works in almost any shop or restaurant.
facts.note: Swap in **저거 주세요** for something farther away. Add a number: **두 개 주세요** = "two, please."

facts.title: When you can bargain
facts.fact: Haggling is welcome at traditional markets and street stalls but not in department stores, chain shops, or supermarkets, where prices are fixed. A polite smile helps a lot.
facts.korean_en: Please lower the price a little.
facts.note: **비싸요** ("it's expensive") is a gentle opener before asking for a discount.

facts.title: Myeongdong cosmetics
facts.fact: Myeongdong is famous for Korean cosmetics, with brand shops packed side by side. Staff often hand out free samples (샘플) and sheet masks to draw shoppers in.
facts.korean_en: I want to buy this cosmetic.
facts.note: K-beauty is a major export. **샘플 있어요?** = "Do you have samples?"

facts.title: Dongdaemun at night
facts.fact: Dongdaemun is a fashion district where some wholesale malls stay open until dawn. Designers and shoppers browse late, and the area buzzes long after midnight.
facts.korean_en: I shop in Dongdaemun at night.
facts.note: **동대문** means "Great East Gate," named after the historic city gate nearby.

facts.title: Lightning-fast delivery
facts.fact: Online shopping in Korea is famous for speed. Many orders placed at night arrive by the next morning, a service often called **새벽 배송** (dawn delivery).
facts.korean_en: The thing I bought yesterday arrived today.
facts.note: **새벽 배송** literally means "dawn delivery" — groceries can land on your doorstep before breakfast.

facts.title: Tax refund for tourists
facts.fact: Tourists can get a tax refund on many purchases at stores marked **Tax Free**. Keep your receipt and passport, then claim the refund at the airport before you fly home.
facts.note: Look for the **택스 프리** (Tax Free) sign. Some shops give an instant refund at the register.

facts.title: Duty-free shopping
facts.fact: Duty-free shops (면세점) sell perfume, liquor, and luxury goods without local tax to travelers leaving the country. You usually pick up your purchases at the airport gate.
facts.korean_en: I bought perfume at the duty-free shop.
facts.note: **면세** means "tax exempt." You'll need your passport and a boarding pass to buy.

facts.title: Second-hand apps
facts.fact: Apps like **당근마켓** (Karrot) let neighbors buy and sell used goods locally. Sellers and buyers usually meet nearby to hand over the item in person.
facts.korean_en: I bought a used bag cheaply.
facts.note: **당근마켓** is named after 당근 (carrot); buying within walking distance keeps it local.

facts.title: Gift wrapping
facts.fact: Gift-giving is taken seriously, and many shops offer free, careful wrapping. Just ask the clerk and your purchase comes back neatly boxed and ribboned.
facts.note: **포장해 주세요** = "please wrap it." The same word covers takeout food: **포장이요** means "to go."

facts.title: Traditional 5-day markets
facts.fact: In smaller towns, **오일장** (five-day markets) open only on dates ending in certain numbers, so they appear every five days. They sell local produce, tools, and regional snacks.
facts.korean_en: The five-day market opens every five days.
facts.note: **오일장** literally means "five-day market." Each town's market follows its own schedule.

facts.title: Trying clothes on
facts.fact: To try on clothing, ask **입어 봐도 돼요?** — "may I try this on?" Shops have fitting rooms (탈의실), though some boutiques limit trying on certain knitwear.
facts.note: **신어 봐도 돼요?** is the same question for shoes (신다 = to wear on the feet).

facts.title: Asking about sizes
facts.fact: Clothing sizes often use numbers (like 90, 95, 100) or the borrowed words S, M, L. To ask for another size, say **더 큰 사이즈 있어요?** — "do you have a bigger size?"
facts.korean_en: Do you have a bigger size?
facts.note: **더 작은 사이즈** = "a smaller size." 더 means "more," placed before the adjective.

facts.title: Cards over cash
facts.fact: Card payment is accepted almost everywhere, even for tiny purchases, and contactless and mobile pay are widespread. Cash is mainly handy at small market stalls.

vocabulary.en: to pay, to settle the bill

facts.note: **카드 돼요?** = "Do you take cards?" — though the answer is almost always yes.

facts.title: Just browsing
facts.fact: If a clerk offers help but you only want to look around, say **그냥 구경하는 거예요** — "I'm just looking." It's a polite way to browse without feeling pressured to buy.
facts.note: **구경하다** means to browse or sightsee, not to buy — handy in any shop.
